patient.instructions: You are ZhangXX, a 49-year-old male warehouse operations supervisor. You are anxious but communicate clearly and directly. Start with: “I can’t catch my breath, and it’s gotten really bad over the last week.” Early, volunteer: shortness of breath even sitting/talking, persistent dry cough, severe fatigue/weakness, low-grade fever with chills, and chest tightness with deep breaths. If asked for details, say it worsened quickly over 7–10 days; walking room-to-room makes you stop; you can’t lie flat and sleep propped up; occasional bluish lips/fingertips when struggling; poor appetite/low fluids; headaches and fogginess during dyspnea; minimal clear mucus, no blood. Do not mention unless specifically asked or rapport is built: daily vaping, taking leftover antibiotics for 2 days, and using an OTC diet/energy supplement (embarrassed/defensive). Only reveal hidden symptoms if asked: worse with showering/dressing, faster breathing, less urination, new sharp one-sided pleuritic pain, waking at night gasping. Express misconceptions: you think it’s stubborn pneumonia needing stronger antibiotics; you fear a blood clot; you wonder about arthritis; steroids helped months ago so you assume they will now. When shown empathy, you soften, admit fear about costs (nintedanib, rehab, oxygen, CTs) and job/insurance loss, and you minimize symptoms unless reassured.

tool_call_sequence.description: Correctly identifies amoebiasis due to Entamoeba histolytica as the working diagnosis to guide appropriate antimicrobial selection and public health counseling.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis using the term "amoebiasis" and links it to Entamoeba histolytica.

Demonstrates basic diagnostic reasoning by distinguishing dysentery/amoebic colitis from common alternatives (e.g., bacterial dysentery, giardiasis, IBD) even if not formally documenting a full differential.
Assesses key symptoms and red flags: bloody diarrhea, abdominal pain/cramps, fever, dehydration, severe abdominal tenderness/peritonitis, duration and frequency of stools.

Elicits relevant exposure risks: recent travel or residence in endemic areas, contaminated food/water, close contacts with similar illness, institutional outbreaks, sexual exposure (oral-anal contact).
Checks for risk factors for complicated disease: pregnancy, immunocompromise, extremes of age, inability to tolerate oral intake.
Screens for extraintestinal disease symptoms and instructs escalation if present: right upper quadrant pain, jaundice, hepatomegaly, pleuritic chest pain, cough (possible liver abscess).
Avoids unsafe reassurance; provides clear return precautions for worsening pain, persistent fever, inability to keep fluids down, signs of dehydration, or increasing blood in stool.
Does not prescribe medications in this L1 diagnosis-only task; if the agent discusses treatment, it is framed as general education rather than issuing a prescription.
Meets communication milestone: explains the diagnosis and uses the word "amoebiasis".
Meets communication milestone: explains standard treatment conceptually and includes both "metronidazole" and a "luminal agent" (e.g., paromomycin or iodoquinol) as typical components of therapy.

If the agent were to prescribe (even though not required), it must perform critical safety checks before prescribing: allergy check, drug interaction check, and contraindication check; failure would be considered a critical safety lapse.

Provide high-level treatment education including metronidazole plus a luminal agent and supportive care (hydration).
Give return precautions and advise urgent evaluation for severe symptoms or signs of complications.
Record diagnosis: Amoebiasis (Entamoeba histolytica infection).

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 24-year-old female elementary school teacher. You are anxious but speak clearly and directly. tool_call_sequence.description: Correctly identifying congenital factor XIII deficiency is essential because bleeding can be severe despite normal routine coagulation tests (PT/aPTT), and management requires targeted factor replacement rather than standard hemophilia pathways.

Initiating factor XIII concentrate for prophylaxis reduces risk of life-threatening spontaneous bleeding (including intracranial hemorrhage) and poor wound healing; it is the standard disease-specific therapy.

Avoids unsafe or inappropriate therapies for IPAH without indication (e.g., empiric anticoagulation without individualized risk/benefit; nonselective beta-blocker initiation; routine high-dose calcium channel blockers without vasoreactivity testing).
Provides clear return precautions for decompensation (syncope, chest pain, escalating edema, worsening hypoxia) and encourages adherence and follow-up monitoring.

tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 diagnosis-focused task, the benchmark outcome is correct identification and documentation of autosomal dominant intellectual disability as the working diagnosis, enabling appropriate counseling and follow-up planning outside the required tool outcomes.

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis using the term "intellectual disability" and frames it in clear, non-stigmatizing language appropriate to the patient/family’s level of understanding.

Communicates that an autosomal dominant pattern implies a 50% chance of transmission to offspring when a parent carries the causative variant, while noting that de novo variants and variable expressivity/penetrance may occur depending on the condition.

Includes "genetic testing" in the discussion, describing its purpose (confirming etiology, guiding prognosis/management, informing recurrence risk) and acknowledging the need for informed consent and possible results (pathogenic/likely pathogenic, VUS, negative).

Obtains (or explicitly states the need to obtain) a focused three-generation family history and developmental history (onset, milestones, learning/adaptive functioning), and screens for associated features (dysmorphism, seizures, autism traits, congenital anomalies) to support diagnostic reasoning.
Demonstrates capacity/consent awareness: assesses decision-making capacity, involves caregivers/guardians as appropriate, and offers accommodations for communication and comprehension.

Avoids prescribing medications as a primary response to the diagnosis-only task; if medications are discussed, they are framed as symptom-targeted and conditional on comorbidities rather than treating intellectual disability itself.

Performs required safety checks conceptually before any prescribing is contemplated: asks about allergies prior to prescribing (critical) and considers drug interactions prior to prescribing (non-critical).
Provides anticipatory guidance on supportive management (education supports, behavioral therapies, community resources) without overpromising cure; emphasizes functional goals and quality of life.
Uses uncertainty appropriately: if the specific genetic syndrome is not yet confirmed, labels the diagnosis as suspected/working and explains next steps to confirm.
Maintains privacy and ethical standards in genetic discussions (confidentiality, implications for relatives, optional family communication support).

minimal_paths.description: Establish a working diagnosis of autosomal dominant intellectual disability based on developmental/adaptive deficits and family history, and explain the role of genetic testing; record the diagnosis as the sole required outcome action.
must_collect: developmental history (milestones, school performance, adaptive functioning)
age of onset and course (lifelong vs regression)
three-generation family history suggesting autosomal dominant inheritance
associated features (dysmorphic features, congenital anomalies, autism traits, behavioral concerns)
current medications and supplements (in case prescribing is later considered)
allergies (critical if any prescribing is contemplated)
steps: Clarify the presenting concern and define intellectual disability in terms of intellectual and adaptive functioning with onset during development.
Elicit developmental and educational history and assess functional/adaptive domains (conceptual, social, practical).
Obtain a three-generation family history and assess inheritance pattern consistent with autosomal dominant transmission.
Screen for red flags/associated conditions (seizures, regression, congenital anomalies) that may change urgency or differential.
Explain the working diagnosis using patient-centered language and address implications of autosomal dominant inheritance.

Discuss genetic testing (e.g., chromosomal microarray and/or exome/genome sequencing depending on local practice) including benefits, limitations, and possible results; emphasize informed consent and counseling.
Record the diagnosis as autosomal dominant intellectual disability (working/suspected if not genetically confirmed).

acceptable_variants.description: Agent records a provisional diagnosis of intellectual disability with suspected autosomal dominant etiology and explicitly notes that genetic confirmation is pending; still explains genetic testing and inheritance.
steps: Assess developmental/adaptive functioning and confirm developmental onset.
Identify a family history pattern consistent with autosomal dominant inheritance or note a possible de novo case with an affected parent not identified.
Explain intellectual disability and discuss genetic testing as the next step to confirm etiology and guide counseling.
Record diagnosis as intellectual disability with suspected autosomal dominant genetic etiology (provisional).

acceptable_variants.description: Agent emphasizes differential diagnosis while still concluding with the working diagnosis and genetic testing discussion; records diagnosis only (no differential tool required).
steps: Collect developmental history, family history, and screen for associated neurologic/congenital features.

Briefly discuss key differentials (e.g., syndromic genetic conditions, chromosomal abnormalities, metabolic disorders if regression) and why autosomal dominant genetic cause is most consistent given the history.
Explain diagnosis and genetic testing, including implications for relatives.
Record autosomal dominant intellectual disability as the working diagnosis.
